case_name: Potable reuse polishing for pesticide-contaminated surface water under high-recovery and concentrate-handling constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for pesticide-contaminated surface water intended for potable reuse, balancing removal reliability against high water recovery and limited ability to manage membrane concentrate.

input_data.scenario_background: A regional utility plans to upgrade a river-derived reuse source that receives seasonal agricultural runoff and treated municipal return flows. The finished water must support potable reuse goals with robust removal of a mixed pesticide load, but the utility has a strong preference for high product-water recovery because of drought conditions. Brine or concentrate disposal options are very limited, making high-pressure membrane systems less attractive even though they are technically capable. The treatment decision therefore hinges on trading off organics removal robustness, operational complexity, recovery, and residuals management.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve stable, potable-reuse-suitable control of the listed pesticides with multiple-barrier reliability and minimized risk of compliance excursions.
secondary_goals: maintain reasonable operator burden for a medium-sized utility
provide resilience to seasonal increases in organic matter and pesticide pulses

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended solids and reduce organic matter load before advanced organics treatment
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish particulates and protect downstream ozone and BAC units
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize susceptible organic micropollutants and convert part of the DOC to more biodegradable fractions
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: adsorb residual pesticides and biodegrade biodegradable organics and oxidation byproducts
core_unit_functions.UV_disinfection: provide final pathogen control barrier for potable reuse

applicability_rationale: the combined ozone-BAC barrier is well aligned with potable reuse applications that need robust trace-organic reduction without generating a large liquid concentrate stream
conventional solids removal up front improves downstream oxidant efficiency and carbon run time for this moderately turbid surface water
high water recovery is achievable because the train does not rely on reject-producing pressure membranes as the primary pesticide barrier
tebuconazole and disulfoton are generally amenable to adsorption and/or oxidation-based control, while BAC provides polishing and dampens seasonal influent variability
this route balances removal robustness with the stated concentrate-handling constraint better than NF or RO
potential_risks: dicrotophos and some oxidation byproducts may require careful ozone dose control and BAC contact time verification
bromate formation risk must be evaluated if bromide is present in the source water
BAC performance depends on bioactivity maintenance, backwashing practice, and media replacement strategy

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove particulates and part of the natural organic matter load
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: provide clarified filtered water to downstream adsorption units
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon: provide flexible seasonal control of pesticide spikes ahead of fixed-bed adsorption
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb residual pesticides and provide polishing barrier
core_unit_functions.UV_disinfection: provide final disinfection for potable reuse

applicability_rationale: an all-adsorptive route avoids concentrate generation and can support very high water recovery
PAC addition offers operational flexibility for short seasonal pesticide pulses often seen in agricultural runoff
GAC provides a second barrier and is a familiar technology for utilities treating trace organic contaminants in surface waters
this option is attractive where concentrate handling dominates decision making and membrane residuals are unacceptable
it ranks below ozone-BAC because DOC is moderately elevated, so adsorption competition may increase media consumption and operating cost
potential_risks: moderate DOC and UV254 can shorten carbon life and increase PAC/GAC demand
spent carbon management and PAC solids handling require planning
removal of more polar compounds such as dicrotophos may be less robust than with combined oxidation plus adsorption unless carbon doses are sufficient

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce particulate and colloidal fouling load before membranes
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: protect nanofiltration membranes and improve flux stability
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: provide strong rejection of many dissolved organic contaminants and additional salinity reduction
core_unit_functions.advanced_UV_AOP: oxidize low-molecular-weight residual organics in permeate and add an advanced oxidation barrier for potable reuse
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: nanofiltration with downstream AOP is technically capable and offers strong multi-contaminant control for potable reuse applications
this route can provide a very high-quality finished water and may be preferred if future contaminant broadening is expected
it ranks below the top options because even high-recovery NF still creates a pesticide-containing concentrate stream that is difficult to manage under the stated constraints
energy demand, fouling management, and membrane operations are materially higher than adsorption- or ozone-BAC-based routes
potential_risks: concentrate disposal is a major project risk given limited off-site options
membrane fouling and scaling can reduce achievable recovery and increase cleaning frequency
if very high recovery is pushed aggressively, concentrate strength and operational instability may worsen

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove solids and lower upstream organic load
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: provide stable low-turbidity feed to advanced treatment
core_unit_functions.ozonation: provide first-stage oxidation of trace organic contaminants
core_unit_functions.advanced_UV_AOP: supply additional oxidation barrier for persistent or residual micropollutants
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: polish remaining organic contaminants and byproducts

applicability_rationale: this hybrid advanced oxidation train is technically strong for difficult trace organic control and creates little to no liquid concentrate
the multiple barriers may appeal where very conservative potable reuse design philosophy is used
it ranks lower because the combination of ozone, UV-AOP, and GAC adds substantial capital, energy use, and operational tuning beyond what is necessary for the stated water quality
the additional complexity is not the best fit when the key constraints emphasize practical high-recovery treatment without concentrate burden
potential_risks: oxidation byproduct management becomes more complex, including possible bromate and biodegradable byproduct formation
higher instrumentation and process control needs increase operator burden
incremental benefit over ozone-BAC alone may not justify cost for this contaminant set

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce solids and membrane fouling potential
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: reject a broad spectrum of dissolved contaminants including many pesticides
core_unit_functions.UV_advanced_oxidation: provide advanced oxidation barrier for RO permeate in potable reuse service
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust corrosivity and mineral balance after RO

applicability_rationale: RO plus UV-AOP is a proven high-end potable reuse configuration and is technically capable of controlling the listed pesticides
it ranks last not because of lack of treatment capability, but because it directly conflicts with the need for high recovery and minimal concentrate handling burden
for this moderately mineralized surface water, RO would impose unnecessary reject management, higher energy use, and greater operational complexity compared with non-concentrating alternatives
this option would become more defensible only if broader dissolved contaminant drivers emerged that clearly outweighed the residuals constraint
potential_risks: significant concentrate stream containing concentrated pesticides creates disposal and permitting challenges
recovery is limited relative to the project's drought-driven goals
fouling, scaling, and chemical cleaning requirements increase lifecycle burden

preferred_rank_pattern: nonconcentrating advanced treatment such as ozone_BAC should rank near the top
activated_carbon_based routes should appear as strong alternatives under high_recovery constraints
NF_or_RO should not rank first because concentrate handling is explicitly constrained
at least one technically capable membrane option should be ranked lower due to mismatch with recovery and residuals priorities

must_reflect: high finished-water recovery is a major decision driver
limited ability to manage pesticide-containing concentrate should penalize membrane-heavy routes
surface water variability requires robust pretreatment and seasonal resilience
potable reuse context justifies multiple-barrier treatment logic

must_contain: why ozone_BAC is attractive for trace organic control without creating concentrate
why activated carbon performance can be affected by DOC competition in surface water
why technically strong NF_or_RO options are downgraded under high_recovery and concentrate constraints
why seasonal agricultural runoff variability matters for process robustness

should_mention: oxidation byproduct formation such as bromate risk
carbon exhaustion or increased media demand due to natural organic matter
seasonal pesticide spikes and variable influent quality
